schools.summary: Paul turns to answer the Corinthians' written questions, the first on marriage; 'to touch a woman' is a delicate idiom for marriage, and he affirms that in certain circumstances celibacy is 'good' (expedient, honourable, advantageous) — though not binding nor superior in itself, as the next verses show; some take the words as a Corinthian ascetic maxim Paul is qualifying (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul does not condemn marriage nor exalt celibacy as holier, but commends the single state as good for those gifted for it, immediately balancing it with marriage's place.

themes: the things whereof ye wrote unto me
It is good for a man not to touch a woman
Paul's opening on marriage and celibacy
celibacy good, not superior

schools.summary: Paul, having granted that celibacy is good, immediately balances it: because of the danger of fornication (the prevalence of sexual sin, especially at Corinth), marriage is the proper and God-ordained provision; each man to have his own wife and each woman her own husband, affirming monogamous marriage as the appointed remedy against incontinence (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): Paul does not present marriage merely as a remedy for lust, but here, against the Corinthian danger, stresses its use in preserving purity; celibacy is not for all.

themes: to avoid fornication, let every man have his own wife
let every woman have her own husband
marriage as God's provision against fornication
the safeguard of purity

schools.summary: Paul enjoins the 'due benevolence' (the conjugal duty, the rights of the marriage bed) which the husband owes the wife and the wife the husband — a mutual debt within marriage; the duty reciprocal, a notable equality of obligation in an age that stressed the wife's subjection (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: marriage carries mutual obligations that neither party may withhold; the conjugal due is owed by both alike.

themes: Let the husband render unto the wife due benevolence
likewise also the wife unto the husband
the mutual conjugal duty
reciprocal obligation in marriage

schools.summary: Paul states the ground of the conjugal duty: the wife has not sole power (authority, control) over her own body, but the husband shares it — and, equally, the husband has not sole power over his own body, but the wife; the reciprocity exact, each having yielded the other a claim, neither wholly independent (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: in marriage the two become one flesh, and this mutual surrender of the body is the basis of the conjugal duty.

themes: The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband
likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife
mutual authority over each other's bodies
the surrender of the marriage bond

schools.summary: Paul forbids defrauding (withholding the conjugal due) one another — neither spouse may deprive the other; the one exception being by mutual consent, for a limited time, that they may give themselves to fasting and prayer (a temporary abstinence for a spiritual purpose, never one-sided or permanent), after which they are to come together again (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): they are to resume the marriage relation, lest prolonged abstinence give Satan occasion to tempt them through incontinency — the safeguard of mutual duty against temptation.

themes: Defraud ye not one the other, except it be with consent for a time
that Satan tempt you not for your incontinency
the rule against withholding the conjugal due
abstinence only by consent for prayer

schools.summary: Paul adds that what he has just said he speaks 'by permission' (as a concession, indulgence) and 'not of commandment' — allowing rather than commanding, leaving liberty; the concession likely referring to the foregoing counsel about marriage and the conjugal relation (vv.2-5), some referring it to the temporary abstinence of v.5 (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul distinguishes his apostolic concessions from binding commands, that the Corinthians might not take his advice as a universal law.

themes: I speak this by permission, and not of commandment
Paul's word as concession, not command
counsel distinguished from command
liberty preserved

schools.summary: Paul expresses his personal wish that all were as he is — unmarried (continent, free for undivided service), esteeming the single state advantageous for those able — but at once qualifies that each has his own gift (charisma) from God: to one the gift of continence, to another the capacity suited to marriage, neither a matter of merit (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): celibacy is a special gift, not given to all nor to be presumed; those without it should marry, and neither state is holier — both are gifts of God.

themes: I would that all men were even as I myself
every man hath his proper gift of God
Paul's wish and the diversity of gifts
celibacy a gift, not a rule

schools.summary: Paul directs counsel specifically to the unmarried (the never-married, or the single generally) and to widows: it is good for them to abide (remain) even as he is — single — commending the single state as advantageous for freedom from care and undivided devotion (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the counsel is for those who can remain continent — good, not commanded, and yielding to the need to marry where continence is lacking (anticipating v.9).

themes: I say therefore to the unmarried and widows
It is good for them if they abide even as I
the counsel to abide single
the single state for the continent

schools.summary: Paul provides for those lacking the gift of continence: if they cannot contain (exercise self-control, remain chaste in the single state), let them marry — marriage being the proper, lawful course; for 'to burn' (to be inflamed with desire, the fire of unsatisfied passion tending to sin) is far worse, and it is better to marry than to be thus consumed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Cyril): marriage is not a lesser holiness but the divinely provided and honourable remedy; those without the gift of continence sin not at all but do well to marry.

themes: if they cannot contain, let them marry
it is better to marry than to burn
the provision of marriage
the honourable remedy for the incontinent

schools.summary: Paul distinguishes this from his own counsel: here he commands not on his own authority but on the Lord's — referring to Christ's own teaching on marriage and divorce (Matthew 19:6-9; Mark 10:11-12); the command being that the wife is not to depart (separate herself, leave) from her husband, the marriage bond not to be dissolved at will (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): this rests on the Lord's express word, the permanence of marriage being his ordinance; the believer is bound by Christ's command, not mere apostolic advice.

themes: unto the married I command, yet not I, but the Lord
Let not the wife depart from her husband
the Lord's command against departing
the permanence of marriage

schools.summary: If a wife does separate (contrary to the command), she has only two lawful options: to remain unmarried, or to be reconciled to her husband — not free to marry another while he lives; and the same binds the husband, who is not to put away (divorce) his wife, the obligation mutual (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): even where separation occurs, the marriage bond remains; reconciliation is the goal, and remarriage is excluded while both live — the permanence of marriage upheld.

themes: if she depart, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled to her husband
let not the husband put away his wife
the rule for separation
the bond unbroken

schools.summary: Paul now addresses a case the Lord had not directly spoken to — mixed marriages between a believer and an unbeliever — giving his own apostolic (yet Spirit-guided, v.40) judgment: if a believing husband has an unbelieving wife content (pleased) to dwell with him, he is not to put her away, difference of religion being no ground for divorce while she is willing (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this concerns maintaining a marriage already contracted, not forming a new one with an unbeliever (which 2 Corinthians 6:14 forbids); the existing bond is to be kept.

themes: to the rest speak I, not the Lord
If any brother hath a wife that believeth not, and she be pleased to dwell with him, let him not put her away
mixed marriages kept where the unbeliever consents
maintaining the existing bond

schools.summary: Paul applies the same counsel reciprocally: a believing woman with an unbelieving husband, if he is content (pleased) to dwell with her, is not to leave him — the rule the same as for the believing husband (v.12); the symmetry showing the obligation holds equally whether the believer be husband or wife, so long as the unbeliever consents (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: the woman which hath an husband that believeth not... let her not leave him
the same rule for the believing wife
reciprocal duty in mixed marriages
equal obligation of either spouse

schools.summary: Not that the unbeliever is saved or made personally holy by the believing spouse, but that the marriage relation itself is sanctified (made lawful, not defiling), so the believer need not separate; and 'holy'/'unclean' of the children means legitimate/illegitimate — if the union were unholy the children would be illegitimate, but they are legitimate, proving the marriage lawful. The verse throws no light on infant baptism; it would spoil the argument to assume the child is baptized (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the children of believers are set apart from others by an exclusive privilege, reckoned holy and in the Church through the blessing of the covenant (as the whole of Abraham's posterity is holy, Romans 11:16); by nature all are alike under sin, but the covenant of salmon made with Abraham's seed is communicated to us — hence if the children are admitted into the Church by the word, why refuse them the sign of baptism? Gill (Baptist) takes the holiness as matrimonial legitimacy only.

Clarke (with Ellicott): the sanctification is a relative, Christian consecration — the children of such marriages reckoned Christian children, received into the Church, not born under the heathen idolatrous rites; the holiness arising from being in the body of Christ, not inward sanctification.

debates.question: Does 'your children are holy' teach the federal/covenantal holiness that grounds infant baptism, or only the legitimacy of the children?

sides.position: Legitimacy — if the union were unholy the children would be illegitimate; the verse gives no support to infant baptism
Federal/covenantal holiness — the children of believers are set apart in the covenant and in the Church, a ground for infant baptism

themes: the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife
else were your children unclean; but now are they holy
the sanctification of the unbelieving spouse
the paedobaptist crux

schools.summary: If the unbelieving spouse insists on leaving (deserting the believer, refusing to dwell together), the believer is to let them go, not compelling the union by force; 'not under bondage' meaning (per Barnes) the believer is not bound to make provision for or to compel cohabitation with the deserter, but is NOT thereby free to remarry — for God has called us to peace, not contention.

Calvin (with Luther, Grotius): the deserted believer is set at liberty — the first and chief bond is not merely loosed but utterly broken through, the unbelieving party having made a divorce with God rather than the spouse; so the believer may marry again, though Robertson notes 'that is by no means clear.'

debates.question: Does the believer's freedom when deserted by an unbeliever permit remarriage?

sides.position: No — 'not under bondage' frees only from compelling cohabitation, not from the marriage bond; remarriage is not permitted
Yes — the bond is utterly broken by desertion, so the deserted believer may marry again

themes: if the unbelieving depart, let him depart
A brother or a sister is not under bondage in such cases
the Pauline privilege
the remarriage crux

schools.summary: The verse may bear two senses. Barnes and Calvin read it as a motive to remain together — the believing spouse may, by example and prayer, be the means of the unbeliever's conversion ('how knowest thou but thou mayest save thy husband?'); Ellicott and Robertson read it as supporting v.15 (letting the unbeliever depart), the hope of conversion being too uncertain to risk the loss of peace by forcing the union. The syntax allows both.

Calvin: it is a great blessing if a believing wife gains her husband; so long as salvation is doubtful, she ought to try every means before leaving him, for God can raise even the dead — though the saving is God's work through her as his instrument.

themes: what knowest thou, O wife, whether thou shalt save thy husband
the uncertain hope of the unbeliever's salvation
the believing spouse's saving influence
remain or separate

schools.summary: Paul lays down a general principle: each is to walk (live, conduct himself) in the station (lot, condition) God has distributed to him and in which the Lord called him — not restlessly seeking to change his outward circumstances upon conversion; and this is no local rule but his settled order in all the churches (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): Christianity does not require men to abandon their lawful station, trade, or social condition; grace is to be lived out where God has placed one — different ranks ordered by God's providence.

themes: as God hath distributed to every man, as the Lord hath called every one, so let him walk
so ordain I in all churches
abiding in one's God-assigned calling
contentment in one's lot

schools.summary: Paul applies the principle of v.17 to the chief Jewish/Gentile distinction: the Jew called as circumcised is not to seek to remove the mark (as some did by surgery to appear Greek), and the Gentile called uncircumcised is not to be circumcised; neither circumcision nor its absence matters to one's standing in Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: these outward marks are now indifferent; to alter one's state in pursuit of them is needless restlessness — the convert is to remain as he was.

themes: Is any man called being circumcised? let him not become uncircumcised
Is any called in uncircumcision? let him not be circumcised
outward condition unchanged at conversion
the indifference of external marks

schools.summary: Paul declares both circumcision and uncircumcision to be 'nothing' — of no account, indifferent, in respect of one's acceptance with God under the gospel; what truly matters is the keeping of God's commandments — obedience, faith working by love (Galatians 5:6) — not the external rite (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the gospel transfers all weight from ceremonial marks to real obedience and a renewed heart; the outward is nothing, the inward and practical everything.

themes: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing
but the keeping of the commandments of God
obedience above outward marks
the true substance of religion

schools.summary: Paul repeats the governing principle (from v.17): every man is to abide (remain) in the same calling (the station, condition, vocation) in which he was called to Christ — not making conversion a pretext for restless change; 'calling' here meaning one's outward condition or occupation, not the inward call of grace (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this does not forbid all change of circumstance, but condemns the restlessness that despises one's lot as if it were unworthy of a Christian.

themes: Let every man abide in the same calling wherein he was called
the rule of abiding in one's calling
contentment in one's station
conversion no pretext for restlessness

schools.summary: Paul addresses the slave (doulos) converted in bondage: let it not be a source of anxiety; servitude is no bar to being a good servant of Christ, and he is to bear his lot with a Christian spirit, never seeking freedom by violence or insurrection. 'Use it rather' — Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Wesley: if you can lawfully obtain freedom, take it; embrace the opportunity, freedom being preferable.

Ellicott reads 'use it rather' the opposite way: so far from letting servitude distress you, prefer to use your present condition (your converted slavery) for God's glory, not seeking change — most in accord with the chapter's drift of abiding in one's calling and with Paul's universal practice (cf. Philemon).

Calvin: leans to the freedom reading — if in place of servitude you could attain liberty, it would be more advantageous; yet the chief aim is to satisfy the slave's conscience, that servitude is no hindrance to serving God.

debates.question: Does 'use it rather' mean use freedom (if you can be free) or use your present servitude?

sides.position: Use freedom — if you can lawfully be made free, embrace it
Use your present servile condition for God's glory, not seeking change

themes: Art thou called being a servant? care not for it
but if thou mayest be made free, use it rather
the counsel to the converted slave
the freedom or servitude crux

schools.summary: The paradox: the slave converted in the Lord is the Lord's freedman (freeman) — though a bondservant outwardly, he is spiritually free, emancipated from sin by Christ; and the free man called to Christ is Christ's bondservant (doulos), though outwardly free, belonging wholly to Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this levels the outward distinction — the slave has true liberty in Christ, the free man true service to Christ; so the slave need not be cast down nor the free man proud.

themes: he that is called in the Lord, being a servant, is the Lord's freeman
he that is called, being free, is Christ's servant
the spiritual paradox of freedom and bondage
outward distinctions levelled in Christ

schools.summary: Paul grounds the exhortation in redemption: believers were bought with a price (the precious blood of Christ, 6:20) — purchased to be Christ's own; so they are not to become the servants (bondslaves) of men, especially not in conscience and soul, yielding to men what belongs to Christ alone (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: while outward servitude is to be borne, the believer's conscience is free, owing supreme allegiance to Christ; he must not enslave his soul to human authority against his Lord.

themes: Ye are bought with a price
be not ye the servants of men
the redeemed not servants of men
supreme allegiance to Christ

schools.summary: Paul concludes this section by repeating the principle a third time: let every man abide (remain) in the condition wherein he was called — but with the crowning addition 'with God': the believer is to remain in his station in fellowship with God, living to his glory, the outward condition mattering little if one abides with God in it (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: every lawful station, however humble, can be filled 'with God'; his presence sanctifies the lowliest lot.

themes: let every man, wherein he is called, therein abide with God
the rule of abiding with God
God's presence sanctifying every station
contentment in communion with God

schools.summary: Paul turns to the case of 'virgins' (the unmarried, or marriageable young people) and notes he has no express command of the Lord on this point; so he gives his own judgment (opinion, counsel) — yet not lightly, for he speaks as one made faithful (trustworthy) by the Lord's mercy, his advice carrying apostolic, Spirit-guided weight (v.40, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul distinguishes counsel from command, yet his counsel is that of a faithful apostle, made trustworthy by the Lord's mercy, and so to be heeded.

themes: concerning virgins I have no commandment of the Lord
I give my judgment, as one that hath obtained mercy of the Lord to be faithful
Paul's judgment concerning virgins
counsel of a faithful steward

schools.summary: Paul grounds his counsel in 'the present distress' (the impending or current persecutions, hardships, and troubles facing the church) — a time of difficulty in which the single state was more advantageous; he judges it good, in such circumstances, for a man to remain unmarried, the single life sparing the added burdens of family in a time of trial (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): Paul's preference for celibacy here is grounded in circumstances (the present distress), not in any intrinsic superiority or higher holiness — a counsel of expediency, not of merit.

themes: this is good for the present distress
it is good for a man so to be
the counsel for the present distress
celibacy by expediency, not merit

schools.summary: Paul applies the principle of abiding (v.20) to marriage: if you are bound to a wife (married), seek not to be loosed (do not seek divorce or separation); if you are loosed (unmarried, single, or widowed), seek not a wife — remain as you are; the counsel cutting both ways, neither state to be restlessly changed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the counsel against seeking a wife is for the circumstances of distress and is not absolute, as the next verse shows that marrying is no sin.

themes: Art thou bound unto a wife? seek not to be loosed
Art thou loosed from a wife? seek not a wife
remaining as one is, bound or loosed
steadiness in either state

schools.summary: Paul expressly guards his counsel: though he advises remaining single in the distress, marrying is no sin — neither for the man nor for the virgin; marriage remains lawful and honourable. Yet he warns the married will have 'trouble in the flesh' — the cares, burdens, and tribulations of family life, especially heavy in a time of persecution and distress (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: 'I spare you' shows Paul would spare them these added troubles by his counsel, out of tender concern, not from any disparagement of marriage.

themes: if thou marry, thou hast not sinned
such shall have trouble in the flesh: but I spare you
marrying no sin, though it brings trouble
marriage lawful and honourable

schools.summary: Paul's great motive: the time (the remaining season, the opportunity of this life and age) is short (contracted, shortened) — whether by the nearness of persecution, of death, or of Christ's coming; so even the married are called to hold their relationships loosely, not neglecting duty but not absorbed in earthly ties, since all is passing (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the believer is to use earthly relations without being engrossed by them, the heart set on eternal things; the shortness of time relativizes all worldly attachments.

themes: the time is short
they that have wives be as though they had none
the shortness of the time
holding earthly ties loosely

schools.summary: Paul continues the theme of detachment: those who weep (in grief, affliction) are to be as though they wept not (not overwhelmed by passing sorrow); those who rejoice (in earthly gladness) as though they rejoiced not (not absorbed in fleeting joy); and those who buy (acquire property) as though they possessed not (using goods without clinging, since all is transitory, Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the believer is to use earthly things — sorrow, joy, possessions — without being engrossed by them, holding all loosely since the fashion of this world passeth away.

themes: they that weep, as though they wept not
they that buy, as though they possessed not
holding sorrow, joy, and possessions loosely
detachment from passing things

schools.summary: Those who use this world (its goods, relations, comforts) are not to 'abuse' it — not to over-use, misuse, or be engrossed by it, but to use it lawfully without excess or absorption; for the 'fashion' (schema, the outward form, the passing scene, as of a stage-show or shifting scenery) of this world is passing away, transitory and soon to vanish (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the transience of the world is the reason for holding it loosely; the believer uses present things as a traveller, not a settler, the heart fixed on what abides.

themes: they that use this world, as not abusing it
the fashion of this world passeth away
using the world without abusing it
the transience of the world

schools.summary: Paul's desire is to have them 'without carefulness' (free from anxiety, distracting cares) — the great reason behind his counsel about the single state; the unmarried man being free to care for (be devoted to) the things of the Lord, how he may please him, his attention undivided, not drawn off by family concerns (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the single state, rightly used, affords freedom for undistracted devotion to God; this, not any superior holiness, is its advantage.

themes: I would have you without carefulness
He that is unmarried careth for the things that belong to the Lord
the unmarried man's freedom for the Lord
undistracted devotion

schools.summary: The married man, by contrast, cares for 'the things of the world' — the temporal affairs, the providing for and pleasing of his wife and family — his attention necessarily divided; no fault but a proper duty, yet this draws off some of the attention the single man can give wholly to the Lord (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the division is not sin but the natural burden of the married state; Paul notes it not to condemn marriage but to show why the single state is freer in a time of distress.

themes: he that is married careth for the things that are of the world
how he may please his wife
the married man's divided care
the burden of the married state

schools.summary: Paul applies the same distinction to women: there is a difference between the married wife and the unmarried virgin in respect of their cares and devotion; the unmarried woman free to care for the Lord's things, that she may be holy (consecrated, devoted) in both body and spirit, while the married woman has the divided care of pleasing her husband and household (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the unmarried woman is free to be wholly given to God's service in body and spirit; the division of the married is the natural burden of that state, not its condemnation.

themes: difference also between a wife and a virgin
the unmarried woman careth for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit
the single woman's freedom for the Lord
the contrast applied to women

schools.summary: Paul disavows any wish to bind them: he speaks all this for their own advantage (profit, benefit), not to 'cast a snare' (a noose, a net) upon them — not to entangle their consciences or impose celibacy as a binding obligation; his whole aim being what is comely (seemly, fitting) and that they may wait on the Lord without distraction (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): undivided devotion is the goal, freely chosen, not compelled; Paul lays no necessity on them but commends what is most conducive to attending on the Lord.

themes: this I speak for your own profit; not that I may cast a snare upon you
that ye may attend upon the Lord without distraction
Paul's loving purpose
undistracted devotion freely chosen

schools.summary: Gill, Henry (with Calvin): 'his virgin' is a father's (or guardian's) unmarried daughter — if he thinks he behaves unbecomingly toward her by keeping her single past the flower of her age (to her discredit, it being then a reproach for a woman to remain unmarried), and need requires, let him give her in marriage; he sins not, and does well, while keeping her single (with her consent) is better in the present distress.

The modern reading (Robertson, Ellicott, and Barnes noting it) takes 'his virgin' of a man and his own betrothed (or one to whom he is engaged): if he thinks he treats her uncomely by withholding marriage, and passion so requires, let them marry — he sins not; the substance being the same, that marrying in such circumstances is lawful and no sin.

debates.question: Is 'his virgin' a father's unmarried daughter or a man's own betrothed?

sides.position: A father (or guardian) and his unmarried daughter, whom he may give in marriage
A man and his own betrothed virgin, whom he may marry

themes: if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely toward his virgin
let them marry
the counsel about a man and his virgin
the father or betrothed debate

schools.summary: Paul commends the man who stands firm in his heart, under no necessity (no compelling circumstance, such as the virgin's strong desire to marry or danger of sin), master of his own will, and who has firmly decided in his heart to keep his virgin (daughter or betrothed) unmarried — and in so doing 'doeth well' (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the resolve to keep the virgin single is commended where it is free, deliberate, and without necessity — good, as marrying is also good (v.38); the choice left to a settled, unconstrained judgment.

themes: he that standeth stedfast in his heart, having no necessity
hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin, doeth well
the steadfast resolve to keep the virgin single
the free choice for celibacy honoured

schools.summary: The one who gives his virgin in marriage (or marries) 'doeth well' — the course good, lawful, and right; while the one who keeps the virgin single 'doeth better' — better in the circumstances of the present distress, for the freedom from care and undistracted devotion already described, not in any intrinsic holiness (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the 'better' is one of expediency for the time, not of moral or spiritual superiority; against the notion that celibacy is a holier state, Paul grounds the preference wholly in present circumstances.

themes: he that giveth her in marriage doeth well
he that giveth her not in marriage doeth better
giving does well, withholding does better
celibacy preferred for circumstance, not merit

schools.summary: The marriage bond holds for life: the wife is bound (by the law of marriage) to her husband as long as he lives, the tie dissolved only by death; on his death the widow is free to remarry whom she will — remarriage of widows being lawful and permitted (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin (with Henry): the one restriction is that she marry 'only in the Lord' — a believer, a Christian, and in a manner consistent with her faith; the liberty is real but bounded by godliness.

themes: The wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth
if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will; only in the Lord
the wife bound for life, free to remarry in the Lord
remarriage bounded by faith

schools.summary: Paul gives his judgment (opinion) that the widow is 'happier' (more blessed, more free from care) if she remains as she is — unmarried — for the reasons of freedom and undistracted devotion already given; and closes by affirming that he too has the Spirit of God, his judgment though modestly offered ('I think') being no mere private opinion but the counsel of an apostle guided by the Holy Spirit (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul, with apostolic modesty, claims the Spirit's guidance for his counsel, that the Corinthians might heed it as Spirit-taught, not lightly dismiss it.

themes: she is happier if she so abide, after my judgment
I think also that I have the Spirit of God
the widow happier single
the Spirit's authority behind Paul's counsel
